title: Clean Architecture in Practice: what really changes in daily work
summary: There is a lot of talk about Clean Architecture, but the real impact goes beyond well-organized folders. Here, I share what actually changed in my workflow after adopting Use Cases and Value Objects in a real ERP.
content:
Why did I pursue Clean Architecture?

Working with legacy systems teaches you one thing fast: code without clear boundaries becomes a trap. A new field in the database requires changes in 5 different places. A validation bug disappears and reappears in another module. It was in this scenario that I decided to study and apply Robert C. Martin's principles seriously.

What is a Use Case, really?

A Use Case is not just a class with an execute() method. It is the isolation of the business intention. When you write IssueInvoiceUseCase, you are saying: "here and only here lies the rule to issue an invoice". This changes how you test, how you scale, and how you onboard someone new to the team.

Value Objects: the end of loose primitives

Before, a CNPJ [Brazilian company ID] was a string. Then, it became Cnpj — an object that validates itself upon construction. It seems like bureaucracy, but it eliminated an entire class of bugs that I didn't even realize I was creating.

What changed on a daily basis

  • Pull requests became smaller and more focused
  • Unit tests became obvious to write
  • New devs understood the flow without needing a 2-hour call

It is not a silver bullet. It has an initial cost. But in systems that grow, it is the investment that pays off the most.

title: How I used AI to automate a radio station's news curation
summary: A real challenge: a morning radio station needed ready-to-use scripts at 5 AM every day. The solution was a pipeline with AI APIs that transformed hours of manual work into minutes.
content:
The problem

Local radio stations have a brutal production pace. The team had to arrive at 5 AM, read news from dozens of sources, filter what was relevant to the region, and also write the scripts in a radio tone. Every day.

The solution's architecture

I built a Node.js pipeline that, at midnight, fetches news from public APIs and RSS feeds, filters them by geographic and thematic relevance, and sends the raw content to the OpenAI API with a prompt calibrated for the station's tone. The output is a text script ready to be read.

What I learned about prompts for professional use

The biggest lesson was that prompt engineering for production is different from experimentation. You need consistent outputs, not creative ones. This means examples in the prompt (few-shot), low temperature, and output validation before delivery.

Result

The team went from 2 hours of preparation to 15 minutes of review. The product evolved into a SaaS with an expansion plan for other stations.
